title: QAnon and the Epistemic Communities of the Unreal: A Conceptual Toolkit for a Sociology of Grassroots Conspiracism
author: Baća

opening_move.description: Existing frameworks pathologize conspiracism as psychopathology, pseudoscience, or parapolitics, thereby depoliticizing it and missing its genuine sociological significance as a form of political meaning-making from below.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A non-pathologizing conceptual toolkit allows us to understand conspiracism as legitimate political practice and epistemic community formation, which is essential for diagnosing contemporary political disalienation.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper rejects three dominant pathologizing frameworks in parallel and proposes three alternative non-pathologizing concepts (cognitive mapping, narrative emplotment, performative citizenship) as a sociological toolkit, introduced through QAnon as case.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Interpretive-conceptual analysis of QAnon as a political-discursive phenomenon; draws on secondary sources and public events (Capitol storming, COVID pandemic) to illustrate concepts.
epistemic_mode.notes: QAnon is used as a 'case-in-point' to illustrate the three analytical concepts, not as primary empirical material. There is no fieldwork, interviews, or content analysis. The argument is conceptual; the case is illustrative.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory, sociology of political knowledge

confidence_notes: The paper's epistemic mode is normative_with_interpretive_case: it builds a theoretical framework and illustrates it through QAnon as a discursive/political object rather than through fieldwork. The 'empirical' moves (referencing Capitol storming, COVID pandemic, Q-drops) are interpretive-illustrative. The normative stake is implicit: pathologizing conspiracism is bad for democratic theory; understanding it sociologically is better.
